text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Discussing Solutions and Strategies

Have a class discussion about the survey data in the Initial Challenge before having students move on to the What If...? questions. The sharing, wonderings, and analyzing of the class strategies for answering the survey questions may help students make sense of and critique the What If...? questions.

Teaching Aid 4.1: Keeya's Tape Diagram from Problem 4.1 can be used to show a visual of the survey data that is in the Student Edition. Note that the numbers in the survey data are set up to make students analyze the relationships. The tacos for lunch results are both 136, with different numbers of students responding to the survey. The favorite class results both have the same number of students responding to the survey, with different number of students saying "yes." The dress code results will require students to both scale up and scale down.

Many students may use equivalent ratios to answer the questions. As questions about percents to help students see the connections in the thinking .

Suggested Questions

  • Did anyone use ratio reasoning to compare the survey data? When did you use it? Why does that type of reasoning make sense in this situation?
Answers will vary. For example, students may scale the tacos for lunch results to 100 to answer thee question.
• Did anyone use percents to compare the survey data? When did you use them? Why does that type of reasoning make sense in this situation?
Answers will vary. For example, students may find the percentages for the favorite class results.
• Did anyone use a tape diagram like Keeya's to compare the survey data? How is that strategy helpful?
Answers will vary. For example, students may say that the diagram is a visual that helps them to think about the partitioning of the percents.
• Did anyone reason about the quantities without doing one of the strategies that we have mentioned so far?

Answers will vary. For example, students may say that with the taco results, you can analyze the ratios without changing the form. Because you have the same number of students who responded "yes," you can just consider the number of students who responded. An amount for every 400 will be much less than an amount for every 200.

Distribute Learning Aid 4.2: Mr. Satyam's Student Strategies to partner group. Give groups a few minutes to analyze What if...? Situation A. There are four student strategies presented. Have groups compare the work to the work of the class. Make sure to have discussions about any strategies that are new to the discussion.

  • Was your thinking most like Suri's strategy, Zane's strategy, Julia's strategy, or Keke's strategy?
  • How are Suri's strategy, Zane's strategy, Julia's strategy, and Keke's strategy alike and different?

Making the Mathematics Explicit

Help students to see why percent is commonly used to compare two comparison statements.

Suggested Questions

  • How are percents useful when you are comparing two different results from the same survey question?

Answers will vary. Students should mention that percents are helpful because you have one quantity in the comparison (100) that is the same. That makes it easier to compare the two quantities. This made it easy to compare the taco data, because the comparison statements had one quantity the same (136 students said "yes"). Or the favorite class data has one quantity in each ratio that is 300. Also, this is why unit rates are nice to compare to ratios, because one of the quantities in each ratio is 1.)
